sentences.text: My name is Pepa Rosenman.
R-O-S-E-N-M-A-N.
I was born the fourth of September 1914 in Przemysl, Poland.
So we were quite a large family.
It means that I had-- we were six children at home, three girls and three boys.
I was the one-- the fifth one.
Afterwards was a-- a boy, a young brother.
And my mother and my sister-- and my father and the grandparents from both sides.
So it was quite a large gathering when we were all together.
You want [INAUDIBLE]-- my eldest sister Ella was quite older-- 10 years older than I was.
Then was my brother, Joseph.
Then was my sister, Rosa, my brother, David, then was-- I was the fifth one, and Henry, the youngest brother.
The second time when I realized that I am different that I got very friendly in the first year of primary school with two girls.
And we played around and [INAUDIBLE] in school, and we liked each other.
So they invited me once to their home.
And we spent a very lovely afternoon.
The next day, they told me, we are very sorry, but you can't come-- we can't play with you anymore.
Then we all went, of course, to Polish schools, all kids.
And that is where the first breach in the tradition come-- came in because we started to write on Shabbat.
We excited to go to school on Shabbat.
Other-- all other holy days-- Jewish holy days-- we were strictly observing at home.
But Shabbat, we had to go to school.
Then at 10, I finished the primary school, and I-- I had to pass an exam to go to the high school.
That was the way that it was in my time, and it is in Poland at 10.
So I passed the exam.
I went to high school, again, to the Polish high school-- the same high school which one sister and the other sister went before.
That-- When I was 11-- around second year-- I was approached by some older girls to join an organization, Zionist youth movement, Hashomer Hatzair.
I joined it because it was normal in our family.
All my brothers and sisters went through the same organization.
I want to explain that Hashomer Hatzair was then the most popular and the strongest youth-- youth movement.
It was based on the principle of a scout.
It means we had all those activities as a scout movement.
But apart from that, we had ideological background.
It means it was Zionist and socialist.
Zionist, we-- our movement was directed to build Palestine.
It means a home for Jews in Palestine, but it had to be based on socialist ideol-- ideology.
Yes.
Yes.
Good.
My father was a very successful businessmen.
My mother was working most of the time with him.
And he was very often on the [? wire ?] because he had this big business with those-- big business all over the Poland.
But he made always a point on Friday afternoon that he was at home because that was when Shabbat started.
Then my mother was a very unusual and very exceptional woman.
She was-- at this time, she was-- she was very young when she married my father.
But she was helping in the business full-time.
The business was metals-- new and old iron, old building materials.
And then my father brought a machine to make-- you know, in those times, when you wanted to send a parcel or a case or something, you had to seal it with a lead seal.
And he was making those.
He was manufacturing those lead seals.
So it was quite a large business and different-- different directions.
And my mother was helping on the spot when my mother-- father had to go traveling.
And we were at home always, but we had always servants-- maid, cook, handyman around the house, around the properties.
The children-- all of us-- we went to private schools.
Only one went to high school, public high school.
We were all-- we had all private lessons-- music lessons, languages, and whatever it was.
I learned German because German was the language most popular in our part of Poland.
Our part was-- before the war-- under Austrian occupation, and that was the official language.
So all-- my parents, and the whole family, and the family which was brought in come to visit us, we all spoke German.
So it was much easier for me after I was in school because the German was our second language.
I chose as a German.
Then we had-- as it was a very big difference between the ages of my sisters and brothers-- so my eldest sister, for example, she acted as a-- really as a mother because she was there always.
And my mother was in the business.
So even when she was married, I got used to it.
When I needed something, I wrote to her or I told her that I need something.
I did not go to my mother-- only to her.
That was my father.
First of all, our family was always Zionist oriented.
It was-- as long as I remember, it was a Zionist box at home.
It was all Zionist [INAUDIBLE].
Officially, when my father was voting, he voted socialist.
That was a very-- thing-- a thing which was very Przemysl because it was the first and only Jewish member of parliament from Przemysl, Lieberman, which was-- he was socially-- socialist.
So all the Jews voted, not because he was socialist or not, but because he was the first member of parliament, Jewish parliament.
So they were called the [?
Lieberman ?] People.
But as I told you, we were very Jewish and Zionist oriented, the whole family, always.
And my father liked all the things which were new.
He did not have any formal education, not in those times.
But he was intelligent enough to be interested in, for example, from one-- once from a travel, he brought a beautiful encyclopedia, German encyclopedia.
Or he was bringing books on when was-- in our time come to Jewish theater from Wilno.
I was then a young girl.
He took me to see-- and I will never forget.
I saw And I did not understand Yiddish, but I will still-- I still see Ida Kaminska on-- on the stage.
No.
I don't play the piano.
I did not touch it from the day that the Russian came and took away my pianos and all my sheet music.
It was in 1939, and of course, with everything what we went through, that was the last on my mind too.
But I was quite good at it.
At 14, my teacher told my father that she can't-- they can't give me anymore in Przemysl-- that was quite a small town-- that he should send me to the conservatorium of music to Lwow.
Well, it was quite time ago, and my father told, you don't send a child to a big town alone.
And that was it.
And that was [INAUDIBLE].
But I played till-- at home for my own pleasure and for a time.
And now I don't know if I would even remember [LAUGHS] after such a long time.
Yes.
That is very important to describe the town because it has a historical significance afterwards and during-- you will hear afterwards, during the war.
Przemysl is situated in the south in the center between Lwow and Krakaow.
It is divided by a River San.
It was a very nice town, really nice.
Whoever talks even know about Przemysl-- it is a little bit-- was a little bit like here, Sydney, on the hill because we were very close to Karpaty.
It was a very hilly country.
And we were divided nearly evenly between those three nationalities.
Like we were 20-- 20-odd thousand Polish people, 23,000 Jews, and 20-odd thousand Ukrainian.
They lived mostly on the edge of the town.
In the center was the old Jewish quarter.
And most of the Jews who were business people-- they had shops.
They had [INAUDIBLE], and professions-- professions like lawyers and this.
They were in the center of the town.
I mentioned that specially, that-- the River San because afterwards when the Russian and Stalin and Hitler divided Poland between themselves, San was the border.
On the left side, the Polish side, were Germans, and on our side, we belonged to-- we were under Stalin occupation.
So and the river, which divided us, was very easy to cross.
The bridge was still standing.
Anyway, the tine-- town was quite peaceful for a long time, perhaps because it was such a balance between all those three nationalities.
But we did not really know much of the tension between till the two, three years before the war when the pressure of the anti-Semitism, which came from Germany on a-- somehow it did.
It was so strong that you could-- you-- you could see the pickets in front of the Jewish shops.
Don't buy from the Jews or something like that.
And when we started really to feel the government pressure on fiscal and economic on the Jews.
But till-- till then, it was quite peaceful.
We had, for example, as a Jewish community of 20-odd thousand people, we had our own institution.
We had our own council, which was called Kahal, with our president.
And we had our schools.
We had our hospital, Jewish hospital.
We had our bath house, yes, and of course, all the synagogues.
And one of the oldest in Poland-- a beautiful synagogue-- was just opposite us where we lived at the beginning.
Yes.
I went to primary school.

And after the-- those two incidents, which hurt me, everything went quiet, and not-- not only quiet, but the teacher who's tried to divide us in the beginning was very friendly with us because we-- because we were mostly the good pupils, you know, the Jewish kids.
My brother went to business, the oldest one.
But from Rosa down, we were all excellent in schools-- school.
Rosa, for example, was a special-- her special subjects were mathematics and physics.
And my brother, the same.
And my inclination was towards languages, history, and things like this, rather humanistic.
And we were all very good at school.
It means we finished ever year with distinctions and our matriculation was with distinction.
And the problem started when my sister Rosa wanted to go to university.
She wanted to study medicine, and that was the problem in Poland.
Jews were not allowed.
There were a few here and there-- you know, you could count them on-- on one hand-- who somehow through some means, got admitted.
But on the whole, every Jew who wanted to study medicine had to go to a foreign country.
Like some went to Italy.
Some went to France.
And my sister and a lot of them went to Czechoslovakia.
It was the closest one.
